title: What a Stock Is and Why Prices Move
html:
A share is a slice of a business

Before you ever read a chart, you have to know what the line on that chart represents. A share of stock is fractional ownership of a real company. Own one share and you own a tiny piece of the factories, the brand, the cash in the bank, and a claim on a slice of future profits. This is not an abstraction invented by Wall Street — it is a legal ownership stake, the same idea whether you own 1 share or 1 million.

The cleanest way to feel this is to build a company from scratch. Imagine you and some friends start Lemon Co., a lemonade business. An appraiser values the whole operation — stands, recipes, brand, expected summer profits — at $1,000,000. You decide to divide ownership into 100,000 shares. Basic arithmetic gives each share a value of:

Share value from company value
Company value $1,000,000 Shares outstanding 100,000 Price per share $1,000,000 ÷ 100,000 = $10.00

That $10 figure is the company's market value per share at the moment of creation. Multiply it back out — price × shares outstanding — and you recover the market capitalization ($1,000,000). Every public company you will ever trade works exactly this way: Apple's stock price times its share count is what the whole company is “worth” in the market's eyes that second.

Why the price is never frozen

Here is the part beginners get wrong. The $10 is not handed down by an authority. Once Lemon Co. is public, nobody sets the price. Instead, the price is simply the most recent number at which a buyer and a seller agreed to trade. It is the location where supply meets demand, and it updates every time a new trade prints.

Suppose the last trade was $10.00. There is a seller willing to let go at $10.05 and, a moment ago, the best buyer was only willing to pay $10.00. No trade can happen at a single price gap unless someone moves. Now imagine a new, eager buyer arrives who believes summer demand will be huge. They decide they will not wait — they raise their offer and outbid the prior buyer, agreeing to the seller's $10.05. A trade prints. The quoted price ticks from $10.00 to $10.05.

Insight.
A stock price is a continuous auction, not a sticker. Every tick is one new agreement between one buyer and one seller. “The price went up” just means the most recent agreement was struck at a higher number than the one before it.

Nothing about Lemon Co.'s lemons, stands, or bank account changed in that second. Only the balance of who wants in versus who wants out changed. That balance is what a chart actually plots over time.

What pushes the balance around

If price is the meeting point of buyers and sellers, then anything that makes one side more eager moves it. The big drivers:

  • Fundamentals (the business itself). Earnings rise, a new product launches, profit margins expand — buyers grow more eager, sellers demand more, price drifts up.
  • Expectations about the future. Markets are forward-looking. A stock can fall on good earnings if buyers expected even better. You are trading the future, not the present.
  • Interest rates and the economy. When safe bonds pay more, risky stocks must compete for capital, often pressuring prices.
  • Sentiment and flows. Fear, greed, index-fund buying, and forced selling all move the balance regardless of the underlying business.
  • Supply of shares. Buybacks shrink the share count; new share issuance dilutes it.

Below is the simplest possible picture: an orderly uptrend, where on balance buyers were repeatedly more eager than sellers, so each new agreement printed a little higher.

Read that chart as a story of pressure. The rising slope is not magic; it is the visible residue of thousands of tiny auctions in which demand kept edging out supply. When the story flips — sellers turn eager, buyers step back — the same mechanism prints lower, and the trend turns. Master this mental model now and every later lesson on spreads, order books, and patterns will slot neatly into it.

Definition.
Price = the most recent transaction price between a willing buyer and a willing seller. Market cap = price × shares outstanding. The chart is a time-series of that transaction price.
▸Try It

Goal: feel that price is an agreement, not a fact.

  1. Pick any company you know and look up its share price and its shares outstanding.
  2. Multiply them to get market cap. Ask yourself: does the whole company really seem “worth” that?
  3. Watch the live price tick for two minutes. Each change is one new buyer-seller agreement — note how nothing about the business changed in those seconds.
  4. Write one sentence: what would make a buyer eager enough to outbid and tick this stock up tomorrow?

title: Exchanges, Market Makers and Liquidity
html:
The exchange is a matching machine

When you click buy, your order does not fly to a trading-floor human in a colored jacket. It is routed to an exchange (NYSE, Nasdaq) or an electronic venue, which runs a matching engine — software whose entire job is to pair buy orders with sell orders according to strict rules. The two rules that matter:

  • Price priority. The highest bid and the lowest offer get matched first.
  • Time priority. Among equal prices, whoever placed their order first gets filled first.

The exchange itself takes no view on the stock. It is a neutral referee that matches willing buyers to willing sellers and broadcasts the resulting prices to the world. The two key numbers it broadcasts are the best bid (highest price anyone will pay right now) and the best offer / ask (lowest price anyone will sell for right now).

The problem: buyers and sellers don't arrive together

Real markets have a timing problem. You want to buy AAPL at 10:00:00.000, but the next natural seller might not show up for another 400 milliseconds. If every trade had to wait for a perfectly matched counterparty, you would sit there unable to transact. This is where market makers come in.

A market maker is a firm that continuously posts both a bid and an offer, standing ready to buy from sellers and sell to buyers at any moment. They are the always-on counterparty. Because they quote both sides, they manufacture liquidity — the ability to trade quickly without moving the price much.

Definition.
Liquidity = how easily you can buy or sell size without moving the price. High liquidity → tight spreads, big resting size, fast fills. Low liquidity → wide spreads, thin size, slippage.

How a market maker actually earns

Market makers are not charities; they get paid through the spread — the gap between their bid and their offer. Watch one work a round trip in AAPL:

Market maker round trip · AAPL · 100 shares
MM posts 189.40 bid / 189.42 ask Seller hits the bid MM buys 100 @ 189.40 = pays $18,940 Buyer lifts the ask MM sells 100 @ 189.42 = gets $18,942 Spread captured $0.02 × 100 = $2.00

The market maker bought at 189.40 and sold at 189.42, pocketing the 2-cent spread — $2 on the round trip — without ever caring which way the stock goes, as long as buyers and sellers keep arriving. Do that thousands of times a second across thousands of symbols and the pennies compound into a real business. Their edge is volume and speed; your job as a trader is to understand that you are paying that spread on the other side of every trade.

Liquidity is not uniform

Mega-caps like AAPL trade millions of shares a day, so dozens of market makers compete, the spread is razor-thin (often a single penny), and you can move size instantly. A tiny micro-cap might have one half-hearted market maker, a 30-cent spread, and almost no resting size — trade it and you are the volume.

That ranging chart is what abundant liquidity looks like from afar: plenty of resting demand near the lows and resting supply near the highs, so normal-sized orders get absorbed without lurching the price. In a thin name the same chart would be jagged — small orders punching air pockets between sparse quotes.

Pitfall.
Liquidity can vanish exactly when you need it — around news, at the open and close, and in extended hours. Market makers widen or pull their quotes to protect themselves, so the spread you saw at noon may be five times wider at 4:01pm.
Insight.
The headline price you see is the last trade. What you can actually transact at is the bid (if selling) or the ask (if buying). Always look at both quotes, not just the last price.
▸Try It

Goal: see liquidity differences with your own eyes.

  1. Pull up a mega-cap (e.g. a giant tech name) and note its bid, ask, and spread in cents.
  2. Now pull up a small, obscure stock and note its bid, ask, and spread.
  3. Compare: how many times wider is the small-cap spread? That ratio is the liquidity gap you'd pay for.
  4. Estimate the market maker's per-round-trip take on each: spread × 100 shares.

title: The Bid/Ask Spread and What It Costs You
html:
Two prices, always

Beginners think a stock has a price. It actually has two prices at every instant:

  • Bid — the highest price a buyer is currently willing to pay. This is the price you receive when you sell.
  • Ask (offer) — the lowest price a seller is currently willing to accept. This is the price you pay when you buy.

The gap between them is the bid/ask spread. It is the single most overlooked cost in trading because no broker lists it on a statement — yet you pay it on the way in and on the way out.

Formula.
Spread = Ask − Bid. Spread cost per share, round trip = Ask − Bid (you buy at the ask, sell at the bid). Mid price = (Bid + Ask) ÷ 2.

Crossing the spread: a worked round trip

Say ACME is quoted 50.04 bid / 50.10 ask. You want a quick in-and-out trade. To buy now, you must lift the ask at 50.10. To sell now, you must hit the bid at 50.04. Watch what happens even if the stock doesn't move a single cent between your two trades:

Spread cost · ACME · 100 shares · price unchanged
Quote 50.04 bid / 50.10 ask Buy 100 @ ask 50.10 × 100 = $5,010.00 Sell 100 @ bid 50.04 × 100 = $5,004.00 Result −$6.00 (loss with ZERO price movement) Spread 50.10 − 50.04 = $0.06 × 100 = $6.00

The 6-cent spread cost you $6 per 100 shares before the market moved at all. You started underwater. For the trade to break even, ACME must rise 6 cents just to cover the spread — the bid has to climb to 50.10 so you can sell back what you paid.

Insight.
Every market-order round trip starts at a loss equal to the spread. You are not betting on the price — you are betting the price moves more than the spread in your favor. The spread is your starting hole.

The spread scales with size and with width

That $6 was on 100 shares. The damage scales linearly with share count and with how wide the spread is:

Shares6¢ spread20¢ spread
100$6$20
1,000$60$200
5,000$300$1,000

Trade a thin small-cap with a 20-cent spread in 5,000-share clips and you hand $1,000 to the market per round trip, win or lose. This is why scalpers obsess over penny-wide spreads — their edge per trade is tiny, so a wide spread can swamp it entirely.

Spread as a percentage matters more than cents

A 6-cent spread on a $50 stock is 0.12%. The same 6 cents on a $3 stock is 2% — you'd need a 2% move just to break even. Always think in relative terms:

Spread as % of price
$50.00 stock, 6¢ spread 0.06 ÷ 50.00 = 0.12% $3.00 stock, 6¢ spread 0.06 ÷ 3.00 = 2.00% $200 stock, 1¢ spread 0.01 ÷ 200 = 0.005%

The chart below marks where the bid and ask sit around a quietly ranging ACME. Notice the price oscillates inside a band; a market-order trader pays the spread crossing it every single time, while a patient limit-order trader can sometimes collect the spread by resting on the bid.

Pitfall.
The spread you see is for small size at the best quote. Want more shares than rest at the best price and you pay progressively worse prices deeper in the book — the effective spread widens. Lesson 0.5 shows exactly how.
Do this.
Before any trade, write the quote as bid / ask, compute the spread in cents and as a % of price, then multiply by your share count. If that number is large relative to your expected gain, either size down, use a limit order, or skip the trade.
▸Try It

Goal: quantify the spread you'd actually pay.

  1. Pick three stocks at different prices ($5, $50, $300) and record each one's bid and ask.
  2. For each, compute spread in cents, spread as a % of price, and the round-trip cost on 200 shares.
  3. Rank them: which is cheapest to trade relative to its price? Usually the high-priced liquid one.
  4. Decide a personal rule, e.g. “I won't market-order anything whose spread is over 0.3% of price.”

title: Order Types: Market, Limit, Stop and Trailing
html:
Your instructions to the market

An order type is the precise instruction you hand the exchange. Choose wrong and you either fail to get filled or get filled at a terrible price. There are two questions every order answers: what price am I willing to accept, and how long should this order live. Let's run all the major types on one stock, ACME, currently quoted 50.05 bid / 50.10 ask, last trade 50.08.

Market order — speed over price

A market order says: “Fill me right now at the best available price, whatever it is.” It prioritizes certainty of execution over certainty of price. You will get filled almost instantly, but you cross the spread and may get slippage if size is thin.

Market buy · ACME
Quote 50.05 bid / 50.10 ask Action Market buy 100 → lifts the ask Fill 50.12 (50.10 + 2¢ slippage, only 50 shares rested at 50.10)

You wanted in and you got in — at 50.12, two cents worse than the displayed ask because the top of the book was thin. Market orders are fine in deep, liquid names; dangerous in thin ones.

Limit order — price over speed

A limit order says: “Fill me only at my price or better.” A buy limit at 50.05 will never pay more than 50.05. The trade-off: you might not get filled at all if the stock runs away from you.

Limit buy 50.05 · ACME
Order Buy 100 limit 50.05 If price dips to 50.05 filled at 50.05 or better If price runs to 50.30 no fill (you missed it) Bonus resting at the bid, you may COLLECT the spread

Stop order — protection that becomes a market order

A stop (stop-loss) is dormant until price touches your stop price, then it converts into a market order. Set a sell stop at 48.00: while ACME is above 48, nothing happens; the instant it trades 48.00, your stop fires and sells at market — possibly below 48 in a fast drop.

Sell stop 48.00 · ACME
While > 48.00 order sleeps Touch 48.00 converts to market sell Gentle slide fills near 48.00 Gap down to 46 fills around 46.00 (no price protection!)

Stop-limit — protection with a price floor

A stop-limit adds a limit so you never sell below a floor. Written 48.00 / 47.90: at 48.00 it fires, but as a limit that won't fill under 47.90. Safer price — but if ACME craters straight through 47.90, you're not filled and still holding.

Sell stop-limit 48.00 / 47.90 · ACME
Trigger 48.00 Limit floor 47.90 Orderly dip fills 47.90–48.00 (good) Gaps to 46 NO FILL — price below 47.90, you stay long into the crash

Trailing stop — a stop that follows price up

A trailing stop sets the stop a fixed distance (5%, or $2.50) below the peak and ratchets up as price rises, never down. It locks in gains automatically. With a 5% trailing stop bought at 50:

5% trailing stop · ACME
Buy 50.00 stop = 50.00 − 5% = 47.50 Rises to 60 stop ratchets to 60 − 5% = 57.00 Rises to 70 stop ratchets to 66.50 Falls to 66.50 sells — locked in ~$16.50/share gain

Notice on that uptrend how the trailing stop sits comfortably below the advance, doing nothing while the trend is healthy, then snapping the position out the moment a 5% reversal arrives — no emotion, no second-guessing.

Time-in-force: how long the order lives

  • Day order — expires at the close if unfilled. The default.
  • GTC (good-til-canceled) — stays working across days/weeks until filled or you cancel (brokers usually cap at 60–90 days).
Pitfall.
A GTC limit can fill on a gap you forgot about. Leave a GTC buy at 45 for weeks; one morning bad news gaps ACME to 44 and you're filled at 45 into a falling knife. Review your resting orders.
Insight.
Use limits to enter (control your price), stops to exit losers (cap risk), and trailing stops to exit winners (protect gains). Reserve plain market orders for liquid names where speed truly matters.
▸Try It

Goal: map each order type to a goal.

  1. For a stock you watch, write its current bid/ask.
  2. Draft five orders on paper: a market buy, a limit buy 0.1% below the ask, a 4% sell stop, a stop-limit with a 0.2% gap, and a 5% trailing stop.
  3. For each, state the exact scenario where it helps you and the scenario where it hurts you.
  4. Pick which time-in-force (day vs GTC) you'd attach and why.

title: The Order Book and Reading Level 2
html:
From one quote to the whole book

Level 1 data shows you a single bid and a single ask — the best of each. Level 2 (the depth-of-book or “the ladder”) shows you all the resting orders stacked at every price, with the size sitting at each level. It is an X-ray of supply and demand: where buyers are lined up below and where sellers are stacked above.

Definition.
The order book is the live list of all resting limit orders. Bids stack below the price (resting demand); asks/offers stack above (resting supply). The top bid and top ask form the current spread.

Reading a ladder

Here is a snapshot for ACME. The best bid is 49.98, the best ask is 50.02 — a 4-cent spread. Read the bid side as “how much demand is waiting below” and the ask side as “how much supply is stacked above.”

Level 2 ladder · ACME
SIZE PRICE SIZE bids asks 50.20 20,000 ← wall 50.10 3,000 50.05 1,500 50.02 800 ← best ask 5,000 49.98 ← best bid 2,200 49.95 1,000 49.90 8,000 49.80 ← bid support

Two features jump out. First, a 5,000-share bid at 49.98 and a thick 8,000-share bid at 49.80 — substantial demand waiting underneath, a cushion that can absorb selling. Second, a 20,000-share offer wall at 50.20 — a heavy block of supply that price will struggle to push through. Traders read these as a near-term floor (49.80) and ceiling (50.20).

What a market order sweeps through

This is the practical payoff of reading L2. Suppose you send a market buy for 4,000 shares. A market order eats the cheapest available supply first, climbing the ask ladder until it's filled. It does not get one clean price — it gets a blended fill:

Market buy 4,000 · sweeping the offers
Fill 1 800 @ 50.02 = $40,016 Fill 2 1,500 @ 50.05 = $75,075 Fill 3 1,700 @ 50.10 = $85,170 (of the 3,000 resting) Total 4,000 shares = $200,261 Avg price 50.0653 vs best ask 50.02 slippage = 4.5¢/share = $181

You wanted 50.02 and got an average of 50.065 — the order walked the book three price levels deep. That extra 4.5 cents × 4,000 shares is $181 of slippage, all because the top of the book was thin. Had you eyeballed the ladder first, you'd have used a limit at 50.05 and refused to chase past it.

Insight.
A market order's true cost is set by the depth of the book, not the headline spread. Reading L2 tells you in advance how far your order will walk — and therefore whether to slice it into pieces or rest a limit.

Walls, spoofs, and what size really means

That 20,000-share offer at 50.20 is a wall. Walls act like magnets and barriers: price often grinds toward them, and a genuine wall absorbs buying and caps the move. But be skeptical — some large orders are spoofs, posted to fake supply or demand and yanked before they fill. A wall that keeps refreshing as price approaches is more likely real; one that vanishes the instant price arrives was probably bait.

That ascending triangle is the chart-level echo of the ladder above: buyers keep stepping up (higher lows off the 49.80 demand), repeatedly testing the 50.20 supply. When price finally trades through 50.20 on volume, it means the 20,000-share wall got bought — supply exhausted — and the breakout can run.

Pitfall.
Don't treat L2 as truth. Hidden/iceberg orders show only a sliver of real size, and spoofers flash fake walls. Use the ladder as one input alongside price action and volume, never as a crystal ball.
▸Try It

Goal: practice reading depth and predicting a sweep.

  1. Open a Level 2 ladder for a liquid stock (many brokers offer it free).
  2. Identify the best bid/ask, the largest resting bid (support) and the largest offer (a wall).
  3. Pick a hypothetical market-buy size and add up the asks it would consume; compute the blended average price and the slippage vs the best ask.
  4. Watch a real wall for five minutes: does it hold, get eaten, or vanish as price nears it?

title: Pre-Market and After-Hours Trading
html:
The market has three sessions, not one

The headline U.S. session runs 9:30am to 4:00pm ET. But trading also happens before and after, on electronic networks (ECNs):

  • Pre-market — roughly 4:00am to 9:30am ET (most activity after 7:00am).
  • Regular session — 9:30am to 4:00pm ET, the deep, liquid core.
  • After-hours — 4:00pm to 8:00pm ET.

Extended-hours sessions exist mainly to let traders react to news that breaks outside regular hours — above all, earnings reports, which most companies release right after the 4:00pm close or before the 9:30am open. The catch: these sessions behave very differently from the regular market.

Why extended hours are dangerous: thin liquidity

Far fewer participants are active, many market makers step back, and order books are sparse. Three consequences follow directly:

  • Wide spreads. A stock with a 1-cent spread at noon might show a 30-cent spread at 5pm.
  • Thin size. A modest order can walk the book several levels and move the price sharply.
  • Gap risk. A few trades on light volume can swing the price violently, and that print may not survive the next session.
Definition.
A gap is a jump between one session's price and the next, with no trading in between. After-hours moves on light volume frequently gap back the other way when the deep regular-session crowd arrives and votes with real volume.

A worked example: the earnings spike that gives it back

NOVA closes the regular session at $100.00. At 4:05pm it reports an earnings beat. On thin after-hours volume, eager buyers chase it up 8%:

NOVA earnings · the round trip
4:00pm close $100.00 4:05pm earnings beat spikes on LIGHT volume 4:30pm after-hours $108.00 (+8%, but spread is 40¢ wide) You chase, buy $108.20 (lift the wide ask) 9:30am next open $104.00 (gap gives back HALF the move) Your mark 108.20 → 104.00 = −$4.20/share (−3.9%)

The stock genuinely rose on good news — yet a trader who chased the 8% after-hours spike is down by the open. Why? The light after-hours volume overshot. When the full regular-session crowd arrived at 9:30am with real liquidity, the price settled at a more sober +4%, giving back half the after-hours move. The chaser paid the wide spread and the gap-back.

Read the chart as two different crowds voting. The tall after-hours candle is a thin vote — a handful of eager buyers with no one to push back. The regular-session open is the deep vote, where size on both sides finds a fairer clearing price. The space between the after-hours high and the regular open is the gap that punished the chaser.

Practical rules for extended hours

  • Use limit orders only. Many brokers force this in extended hours for good reason — a market order into a 40-cent spread is a gift to whoever's on the other side.
  • Respect the spread. If the spread is 40 cents, you need a 40-cent move just to break even on a round trip.
  • Don't anchor to the last after-hours print. It may be one tiny trade; it is not a reliable price.
  • Beware the gap-back. Initial reactions to news routinely overshoot and partially reverse at the next open.
Pitfall.
Holding through earnings means you accept overnight gap risk with no ability to manage it — stop-losses don't trigger between sessions. A stock can open 20% lower than where your stop sat, and you'll be filled far below it. Size positions assuming the gap can blow through your stop.
Insight.
Extended hours are for informed reaction, not casual trading. The same news, traded patiently after liquidity returns at 9:30am, usually offers a tighter spread and a clearer, less emotional price.
▸Try It

Goal: witness the thin-liquidity gap-back.

  1. Find a stock reporting earnings after the close. Note its 4:00pm price.
  2. Check the after-hours quote: record the bid, ask, and spread. Compare the spread to its regular-hours spread.
  3. Note the biggest after-hours price (the spike or drop).
  4. The next morning, record the 9:30am open. How much of the after-hours move held vs gave back? Write down the spread cost a chaser would have paid.

title: Accounts, Settlement and the Hidden Costs
html:
Cash vs margin: two ways to hold an account

Before any of this matters, you trade inside a brokerage account, and the type you choose changes the rules.

  • Cash account. You trade only with settled cash. No borrowing. Lower risk, simpler — but you must wait for trades to settle before reusing the proceeds, which creates the violations described below.
  • Margin account. The broker lends you money against your holdings, letting you buy more than your cash (leverage) and short-sell. Proceeds are also available faster. The cost: interest on the loan, margin calls if your equity drops, and amplified losses.
Definition.
Leverage via margin magnifies both gains and losses. Buy $20,000 of stock with $10,000 cash + $10,000 margin and a 10% drop in the stock is a 20% loss on your equity — plus interest.

T+1 settlement: the trade isn't final the instant it fills

When you buy, the execution is immediate but the settlement — the actual exchange of cash for shares — completes one business day later, T+1 (as of the 2024 U.S. standard). When you sell, the cash from that sale isn't fully settled and free of restrictions until T+1. In a margin account this rarely bites you. In a cash account, reusing unsettled proceeds too fast triggers a violation.

The good-faith violation, step by step

This is the classic cash-account trap. You must let proceeds settle before you sell what you bought with them.

Good-faith violation · cash account
Mon Sell old shares for $5,000 — cash settles Tue (T+1) Mon Use that UNSETTLED $5,000 to buy ACME Mon Sell ACME same day, before Tue VIOLATION — you sold a position bought with funds that had not yet settled (good-faith violation) Penalty 3 violations in 12 months → account restricted to settled cash only for 90 days

The fix is simple: in a cash account, either wait for proceeds to settle (T+1) before selling what you bought with them, or use a margin account where this restriction largely disappears. Beginners hit this constantly because the buying power shows the unsettled cash as available — the platform lets you dig the hole.

The hidden costs: “zero commission” is not free

Most brokers advertise $0 commissions on stocks. Real, but it hides three other costs that quietly drain accounts:

  • The spread (Lesson 0.3) — you pay it crossing the bid/ask every round trip.
  • Slippage (Lesson 0.5) — getting filled worse than the displayed quote when the book is thin.
  • Payment for order flow (PFOF) — how “free” brokers actually get paid.

How PFOF works

Your broker routes your order not straight to the exchange but to a wholesale market maker, who pays the broker a small fee for the right to fill it. The market maker profits from the spread; the broker gets paid; you get a “free” commission. The debate is whether the fill you receive is as good as it could have been. Regulators require “best execution,” but on thin or fast names, fractions of a cent can leak out of your fill — multiplied across millions of orders, that's the whole business model.

Tallying the real cost of one “free” trade

Here is the punchline. A zero-commission market order in ACME (49.98 bid / 50.02 ask, a 4-cent spread) for 1,000 shares, round trip, with 1 cent of slippage per side:

True cost of a 'zero-commission' 1,000-share round trip
Commission $0.00 (the headline) Spread, 4¢ × 1,000 $40.00 (cross bid/ask once each way) Slippage, 1¢ entry $10.00 Slippage, 1¢ exit $10.00 Wait — spread is the round-trip already; count slippage extra Spread cost (round trip) $40.00 Slippage (both sides) $20.00 Total real cost $60.00 on a 'free' trade To break even ACME must rise 6¢/share

The simplest framing: even at $0 commission, the spread alone on a 4-cent quote and 1,000 shares is $40, and a single penny of slippage on entry costs another $10 — so a 4-cent-spread, 1-cent-slip round trip quietly costs around $11 on the entry leg alone and tens of dollars all-in. “Free” describes the commission line, not the trade.

That ranging chart is where hidden costs do the most damage: lots of round trips, small moves, and the spread-plus-slippage toll on every one. Overtrade a quiet range and the cumulative friction — not the market — is what bleeds the account.

Insight.
Cost per trade matters most when you trade often. The friction of spread + slippage scales with frequency, so a high-turnover style needs the tightest spreads and the most patient (limit-based) entries to survive.
Pitfall.
Two traps to remember: (1) in a cash account, reusing unsettled T+1 proceeds can trigger good-faith violations and a 90-day restriction; (2) $0 commission never means $0 cost — spread, slippage, and PFOF are the real price of admission.
▸Try It

Goal: see your true trading costs and settlement rules.

  1. Check whether your account is cash or margin, and find your broker's settlement note (look for T+1).
  2. Take a stock you'd actually trade; record its bid/ask. Compute the round-trip spread cost on your typical share size.
  3. Add a realistic 1-cent-per-side slippage estimate. That total is what the market really charges you, commission aside.
  4. If you have a cash account, write the rule: “Proceeds settle T+1; I won't sell anything bought with unsettled cash until then.”
